questions.passage:
There is no more durable superstition in the history of art than the belief in the solitary genius, the singular figure who conjures masterpieces out of private inspiration while the rest of us look on. It is a flattering story, and a false one. Nearly every work we file under a single celebrated name was in fact the product of many hands, many hours of ordinary labor, and a division of work that the name conveniently erases. The myth persists not because it is true but because it is useful, and it is worth asking whom it serves.

Consider the Renaissance workshop, that busy commercial enterprise we have sentimentally rechristened the studio of a master. The celebrated painter ran a firm. Apprentices ground the pigments, prepared the panels, blocked in the drapery, and finished the lesser figures; the master might reserve the principal face and a few passages of difficulty for himself, or he might not, delegating even those to a gifted assistant while he negotiated the next commission. The signature on the finished work was a trademark, a guarantee of quality issued by the shop, not a confession that one pair of hands had done everything. To read it as such is to mistake a brand for a biography.

The Romantic era hardened this convenient shorthand into a metaphysics. The genius was no longer a skilled proprietor but a vessel of forces beyond the reach of training or explanation, and his productions were held to spring, fully formed, from a wellspring the ordinary person could never tap. This was nonsense dressed as reverence. It severed art from the crafts that actually produce it, and it invited a whole apparatus of worship, the pilgrimage to the birthplace, the veneration of the discarded sketch, the reverent hush before the relic, that has more in common with a cult than with any honest account of how pictures and poems get made.

The costs of the superstition are not merely intellectual. When we credit everything to one anointed name, we render invisible the collaborators, assistants, copyists, models, patrons, and printers without whom the celebrated object could not exist. Whole careers vanish into the master's shadow. The engraver who spread a painter's reputation across a continent, the workshop hand who executed most of a fresco, the wife or sister who ran the studio's accounts and often its brush, are demoted to footnotes or deleted altogether. The single name does not merely simplify; it expropriates.

Defenders of genius will protest that some individuals really are extraordinary, that talent is unequally distributed, that a Michelangelo is not interchangeable with his apprentices. Granted, and beside the point. No one need deny that abilities differ to see that the myth of the self-sufficient creator is a distortion. Exceptional skill is real; the lone wellspring is not. The most gifted figures we know of were embedded in dense networks of teachers, rivals, assistants, and traditions, drawing on inherited techniques and shared workshops at every turn. Strip those away and the supposed genius could not have lifted a brush.

Why, then, does the story survive? Because it is commercially convenient and psychologically consoling. A market that must sell unique objects has every reason to promote the fiction of the unique maker; a public that wishes to believe greatness is a gift from beyond has every reason to accept it. The auction house and the pilgrim collaborate in the same illusion. It would be more honest, and no less astonishing, to describe art as most human achievements are achieved, through cooperation, imitation, correction, and toil. That we prefer the fairy tale of the anointed individual says less about how art is made than about what we would like to believe of ourselves.
questions.stem: The central claim of the passage is that

choices.A: the idea of the self-sufficient creative genius is a distorting myth that erases collaborative labor
choices.B: talent is distributed so evenly across all people that no individual artist ever deserves more credit than a workshop assistant
choices.C: Renaissance workshops produced better art than the Romantic cult of genius later allowed
choices.D: art should no longer be attributed to any individual name under any circumstances

explanations.A: Correct. The author argues throughout that the solitary genius is a false and useful superstition, that celebrated works were the product of many hands, and that the single name expropriates the collaborators. The distortion, and its erasure of shared labor, is the through-line.
explanations.B: The author explicitly grants that abilities differ and that a Michelangelo is not interchangeable with his apprentices; the target is the myth of self-sufficiency, not the existence of unequal talent.
explanations.C: The passage treats the workshop as evidence that art was collaborative, not as a golden age superior to a later one; it makes no comparative quality judgment between periods.
explanations.D: The author objects to reading the name as a full biography, not to attribution as such; the complaint is against crediting everything to one name, not against naming anyone.

questions.stem: The author describes a workshop signature as 'a trademark' in order to

choices.A: praise the master's commercial shrewdness in building a recognizable brand
choices.B: argue that the presence of a signature guaranteed that one master artist had personally executed every part of the finished work
choices.C: show that the name marked a shop's guarantee rather than proof of one artist's whole hand
choices.D: establish that forgery was rampant and signatures therefore meaningless

explanations.A: The trademark point is not admiration for shrewdness; it is an argument that the signature does not mean what the genius myth assumes, which is a critique rather than praise.
explanations.B: This reverses the author's meaning. The signature is called a trademark precisely to deny that one pair of hands did everything; reading it as personal execution is the error the author attacks.
explanations.C: Correct. The author calls the signature a trademark, a guarantee of quality issued by the shop, not a confession that one pair of hands had done everything, and warns against mistaking a brand for a biography.
explanations.D: The passage says nothing about forgery; the trademark analogy concerns divided labor within a legitimate shop, not counterfeit signatures.

questions.stem: The passage's tone is best characterized as

choices.A: detached and noncommittal about competing accounts of artistic creation
choices.B: pointed and debunking, treating the belief in genius as a superstition to be exposed
choices.C: nostalgic for a lost era when genius was properly honored
choices.D: tentative, offering the collaborative account only as one speculative possibility

explanations.A: The author is anything but detached, opening with the durable superstition of solitary genius and calling it flattering and false; the passage takes a firm side.
explanations.B: Correct. The author repeatedly brands the belief a superstition, a myth, and nonsense dressed as reverence, and sets out to show whom the fiction serves. The tone is a sustained, pointed debunking.
explanations.C: There is no nostalgia for an age that honored genius; the author regards the cult of genius as the distortion to be dismantled, not a lost good.
explanations.D: The collaborative account is asserted with confidence as how art is actually made, not floated as one speculative option among others.

questions.stem: The author's concession that 'a Michelangelo is not interchangeable with his apprentices' functions to

choices.A: abandon the argument outright by admitting that the solitary creative genius is real and self-sufficient after all
choices.B: concede that attribution to a single name is ultimately harmless
choices.C: prove that only the most gifted artists ever deserved workshop assistance
choices.D: grant that talent varies while maintaining that the self-sufficient creator is still a myth

explanations.A: The concession does not abandon the argument; the author immediately calls it beside the point and insists exceptional skill is real, but the lone wellspring is not. The thesis stands.
explanations.B: The author holds the single name is harmful, that it expropriates collaborators, so the concession cannot be conceding that attribution is harmless.
explanations.C: The passage draws no conclusion that only the gifted merit assistance; it argues that even the most gifted were embedded in networks and could not have worked alone.
explanations.D: Correct. The author grants that abilities differ and that Michelangelo is not interchangeable with apprentices, then argues that conceding unequal talent leaves the myth of the self-sufficient creator intact as a distortion. The concession isolates what the author does and does not deny.

questions.stem: The passage implies that the pilgrimage to a birthplace and the veneration of a discarded sketch are

choices.A: reasonable tributes to genuinely extraordinary individuals
choices.B: practices that accurately reflect how artworks are produced
choices.C: symptoms of a quasi-religious worship the author regards as misplaced
choices.D: harmless rituals the author endorses despite their inaccuracy

explanations.A: The author presents these rituals as evidence of a cult rather than as fitting tributes, grouping them under an apparatus of worship the passage means to discredit.
explanations.B: The passage says the veneration severs art from the crafts that produce it, so the rituals misrepresent, rather than reflect, how works are made.
explanations.C: Correct. The author lists the pilgrimage, the veneration of the sketch, and the reverent hush before the relic as an apparatus of worship with more in common with a cult than with an honest account of how art is made. The framing is plainly disapproving.
explanations.D: Nothing in the passage endorses the rituals; the author treats them as symptoms of a superstition to be exposed, not as harmless practices to accept.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the myth of solitary genius survives chiefly because

choices.A: the surviving historical evidence overwhelmingly supports the existence of lone creators working in genuine solitude
choices.B: it is commercially convenient for sellers and psychologically consoling for the public
choices.C: scholars have failed to examine Renaissance workshop practices
choices.D: collaborative art has consistently proven inferior in quality

explanations.A: The author argues the evidence cuts the other way, that even the most gifted worked within dense networks, so survival cannot rest on evidence for lone creators.
explanations.B: Correct. The author answers the closing challenge directly: the story survives because it is commercially convenient and psychologically consoling, with the auction house and the pilgrim collaborating in the same illusion.
explanations.C: The passage itself draws on workshop practice to make its case, so it cannot be attributing the myth's survival to scholarly neglect of those workshops.
explanations.D: The author never claims collaborative art is inferior; the argument is that nearly all art is collaborative, which would make such a comparison incoherent.
